Importance of prior patient interactions with the healthcare system to engaging with pre-test cancer genetic services via digital health tools among unaffected primary care patients: findings from the BRIDGE trial

evidence_entities.text: Number of previous patient portal logins (UHealth average marginal effect [AME]: 0.32; 95% CI: 0.27, 0.38; NYULH AME: 0.33; 95% CI: 0.27, 0.39), having a recorded primary care provider (NYULH AME: 0.15; 95% CI: 0.08, 0.22), and more primary care visits in the previous three years (NYULH AME: 0.09; 95% CI: 0.02, 0.16) were associated with opening patient portal messages about genetic services.

Number of previous patient portal logins (UHealth AME: 0.14; 95% CI: 0.08, 0.21; NYULH AME: 0.18; 95% CI: 0.12, 0.23), having a recorded primary care provider (NYULH AME: 0.08; 95% CI: 0.01, 0.14), and more primary care visits in the previous three years (NYULH AME: 0.07; 95% CI: 0.01, 0.13) were associated with beginning pre-test genetic services.
Patient sociodemographic and clinical characteristics were not significantly associated with either outcome.
As system-level initiatives aim to reach patients eligible for cancer genetic services, patients already interacting with the healthcare system may be most likely to respond.
